Clarity.justification: The paper is well-structured with clear sections (Introduction, Related Work, Method, Results, Discussion, Conclusion), explicit research questions (RQ1-RQ3), and precise mathematical definitions (e.g., margin_i = (v_base^(1)(i) − v_base^(2)(i)) / σ, flip_i indicator). Tables (e.g., Table 1 with β₁, AUROC values) and figures are referenced consistently. Minor issues include hardcoded figure paths and occasional repetition of limitations, but language and logical flow are coherent and easy to follow.

Novelty.justification: The work introduces a novel per-item predictor (pre-alignment confidence margin) for argmax instability after alignment, operationalized as a classification task with cross-benchmark generalization (AUROC 0.867–0.909 on held-out TruthfulQA/ARC without retraining). Geometric analysis of logit-delta anisotropy (ratios 2.90 for DPO, 4.58 for SFT vs. isotropic control 1.13) and quintile variance profiles add originality. It builds on prior calibration and geometric work (e.g., Plaut et al., Khanmohammadi et al.) but targets a previously unaddressed gap in item-level pre-deployment risk assessment.

Soundness.justification: Methods are appropriate and rigorously applied: mixed-effects logistic regression (β₁ = −4.33, p ≈ 10^{-227}, η² = 0.289), eigendecomposition for anisotropy, Welch's t-tests with bootstrap CIs, and KL-residualization. Results are supported by data (N=14,042 MMLU items, flip rate 12.5%) and match the provided code (caches, logs, 45 unit tests). Limitations (no PPO pairs due to 404/tokenizer errors, MCQ-only scope, H-M2 directional hypothesis null with reversed quintile trend) are transparently documented. Experimental design is reliable with z-scoring, permutation tests, and sanity checks (isotropic Gaussian ρ=1.13).

Significance.justification: The paper addresses an important problem in LLM trustworthiness—predicting alignment-induced answer changes before deployment—with practical implications for auditing low-margin items (Q1 flip rate ~25% vs. Q5 ~1.5%). Cross-benchmark generalization and non-isotropy findings contribute to the field. However, primary evidence is from one model pair (tulu-2-7b DPO), results are MCQ-specific, two planned analyses (H-M3, H-M4) were not executed, and H-M2 yielded a null/reversed result, limiting broader impact and lasting influence.

strengths: Strong empirical results for margin predictor on DPO (AUROC 0.803–0.909, large effect size) with no domain adaptation
Transparent documentation of limitations, infrastructure failures, and post-hoc observations (e.g., monotone DPO quintile variance increase)
weaknesses: Primary findings rely on a single model pair; SFT results weaker (AUROC 0.609) and PPO unavailable
Scope restricted to 4-option MCQs; generalization to free-form generation unaddressed
